extractions.problem: Understanding how benzothiadiazine drug derivatives interact with phospholipid cell membranes to inform drug design for hypertension, hypoglycemia, and HIV treatments.
extractions.method: Microsecond all-atom molecular dynamics simulations using CHARMM36m force field with DOPC/DOPS/cholesterol bilayer models constructed via CHARMM-GUI; four in silico-designed DBD derivatives with electron-donating and electron-accepting substituents.
extractions.finding: All benzothiadiazine derivatives show strong affinity for the membrane interface and form single/double hydrogen bonds (1.6–2.1 Å) with lipids and cholesterol, though solvation characteristics vary significantly between derivatives (fully water-solvated vs. continuously membrane-attached for 10–70 ns).
extractions.dataset: DOPC/DOPS-K/cholesterol bilayer model (112 DOPC, 28 DOPS, 60 cholesterol, 10,000 water molecules), 5 DBD derivative systems
extractions.metric: area per lipid, membrane thickness, radial distribution functions, hydrogen bond length and occupancy
extractions.baseline: previously simulated DBD1 reference; experimental values from Nagle et al.
extractions.result: A=52.2 Å² (exp 54.4 Å²), Δz≈43.0 Å (exp 40 Å), H-bond lengths 1.6–2.1 Å, <5% fluctuation in membrane properties

extractions.problem: Lack of standardized terminology and systematic criteria for interpreting sequence variants identified through increasingly complex clinical genetic testing including NGS panels, exomes, and genomes.
extractions.method: Expert workgroup consensus (ACMG/AMP/CAP) with two rounds of surveys to >100 sequencing laboratories, open forums, workshops, and iterative testing of classification criteria against known variants.
extractions.finding: Established a 5-tier classification system (pathogenic, likely pathogenic, uncertain significance, likely benign, benign) with 28 evidence-based criteria combining population, computational, functional, and segregation data for Mendelian disorder variant interpretation.
extractions.dataset: surveys from >45 laboratories in North America, protocols from 11 labs
extractions.result: 5-tier system with 28 criteria adopted by majority of surveyed labs; 'likely pathogenic/benign' defined as >90% certainty

extractions.problem: Drug-tolerant CD44v9-positive cancer cell subpopulations in gastric tumors cause early relapse after cytotoxic chemotherapy, and no established treatment strategy exists to eliminate them.
extractions.method: Gene expression signature-based in silico screening using JFCR_LinCAGE anticancer compound database on patient-derived gastric cancer cells, validated in BALB/c-nu/nu mouse xenograft models with flow cytometry, MTT assays, and Western blotting.
extractions.finding: EGFR inhibitors (afatinib, erlotinib) selectively target CD44v9-positive cells at clinically relevant concentrations by downregulating AKT/ERK pathways, significantly reducing residual cancer cells and enhancing irinotecan's antitumor effect in vivo.
extractions.dataset: gastric cancer patient-derived cells (JSC15-3, JSC17-2, JSC17-7), 66 clinical tissue microarray samples, BALB/c-nu/nu xenograft
extractions.metric: cell viability (MTT IC50), CD44v9+ fraction (flow cytometry), colony formation, tumor volume
extractions.baseline: SN-38 or 5-FU treatment alone, DMSO control
extractions.result: CD44v9 in 34.8% of gastric cancers (66 cases), EGFR inhibitors significantly reduced residual cells after cytotoxic treatment

extractions.problem: Bacterial biofilm formation on food matrices and processing equipment causes persistent contamination, and traditional sanitizers have inadequate efficacy against biofilms.
extractions.method: Agar well diffusion, MIC/MBC determination, biofilm disruption assays (crystal violet, XTT, LDH), light/scanning electron microscopy, and molecular docking of identified phytochemicals against bacterial adhesion proteins (Sortase A, EspA, OprD, type IV b pilin).
extractions.finding: E. sativa ethanolic crude extract effectively inhibits planktonic growth and disrupts established biofilms of all tested food-borne pathogens; molecular docking suggests the mechanism involves targeting cell adhesion proteins.
extractions.dataset: S. aureus MTCC 96, E. coli MTCC 9537, P. aeruginosa MTCC 741, S. enterica ser. typhi MTCC 8767
extractions.metric: zone of inhibition, MIC, MBC, biofilm inhibition %, EPS reduction %, XTT cell viability
extractions.baseline: chloramphenicol (1 mg/mL positive control), 10% DMSO (negative control)
extractions.result: MIC 125–500 µg/mL, MBC 250–1000 µg/mL, biofilm inhibition 58.68–73.45%, EPS reduction 59.73–82.77%

extractions.problem: Need for rationally designed dual kinase inhibitors that simultaneously target angiogenesis (VEGFR2) and cell cycle progression (CDK1) for anti-cancer therapy.
extractions.method: In silico molecular modeling (SPROUT, Glide) for dual-target compound design, validated by recombinant kinase assays ([γ-³³P]-ATP), endothelial-fibroblast co-culture angiogenesis model, and murine ex vivo aortic ring assay.
extractions.finding: JK-31 potently inhibits both VEGFR2 and CDK1, blocks VEGF-A-mediated signaling and mitotic entry, displays anti-angiogenic activity in co-culture and ex vivo models, and reduces growth of endothelial and MCF-7 breast cancer cells.
extractions.dataset: human primary endothelial cells, MCF-7 breast cancer cells, murine aortic ring ex vivo model
extractions.metric: IC50, predicted pKi (binding affinity), cell proliferation, kinase inhibition %
extractions.baseline: vatalanib, sunitinib (VEGFR2 inhibitors), AT7519, JNJ-7706621 (CDK1 inhibitors), staurosporine
extractions.result: predicted pKi ≤ -7 (nanomolar affinity) for both targets, greater VEGFR2 than CDK1 potency, greater CDK1 inhibition than AT7519

extractions.problem: Molecular mechanisms of caffeine's anticancer effects on melanoma are not fully elucidated, particularly regarding effects at defined differentiation levels of cancer cells.
extractions.method: In silico screening of caffeine molecular targets in GEO GDS1375 melanoma dataset (ROC analysis, cross-validation via IST Online and GEPIA2), followed by in vitro proliferation, melanin production, and cytokine profiling on BRAF-mutated melanoma-initiating cells (MICs).
extractions.finding: Tyrosinase (TYR) is a key mediator of caffeine's action on melanoma; caffeine promotes melanin production and reduces secretion of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IP-10, MIP-1α, MIP-1β, RANTES) in MICs. Low TYR expression correlates with poor prognosis in BRAF-mutated melanoma.
extractions.dataset: GEO GDS1375 (45 primary melanomas, 18 nevi), BRAF-mutated MICs (Mel1, Mel3)
extractions.metric: AUC-ROC, cell count, melanin content, cytokine concentration (Luminex), overall survival (Kaplan-Meier)
extractions.result: 8 genes with AUC≥0.85 (TYR AUC=0.94, PARP1 AUC=0.98), low TYR significantly associated with unfavorable prognosis in BRAF-mutated melanoma

extractions.problem: Need for effective natural PTP1B and α-glucosidase dual inhibitors for T2DM treatment, as existing synthetic inhibitors have dose-dependent side effects and have failed clinical trials.
extractions.method: Enzyme inhibition assays (PTP1B with p-NPP, α-glucosidase with p-NPG), Lineweaver-Burk and Dixon plot kinetic analysis, molecular docking (AutoDock 4.2), and glucose uptake measurement (2-NBDG) in insulin-resistant HepG2 cells.
extractions.finding: Mulberrofuran G, albanol B, and kuwanon G from M. alba root bark show potent mixed-type dual inhibition of PTP1B and α-glucosidase, enhance glucose uptake, and decrease PTP1B expression dose-dependently in insulin-resistant HepG2 cells.
extractions.dataset: insulin-resistant HepG2 cells, recombinant human PTP1B, α-glucosidase
extractions.metric: IC50, Ki, binding energy (kcal/mol), glucose uptake (2-NBDG fluorescence), PTP1B expression (Western blot)
extractions.baseline: ursolic acid (PTP1B IC50 3.54 µM), acarbose (α-glucosidase IC50 119.16 µM), rosiglitazone
extractions.result: PTP1B IC50: 0.57 µM (mulberrofuran G), 0.80 µM (albanol B), 2.26 µM (kuwanon G); α-glucosidase IC50: 1.31–2.35 µM; binding energy -8.24 kcal/mol (allosteric PTP1B)

extractions.problem: Whipple's disease caused by T. whipplei is rare but fatal, and current treatments (hydroxychloroquine/doxycycline for 12–18 months) are ineffective with lifetime follow-up required; no vaccine exists.
extractions.method: Immuno-informatics pipeline: allergenicity screening (AllergenFP), T-cell epitope prediction (NetMHCII PAN 3.2), antigenicity scoring (VaxiJen ≥0.7), 3D modeling (PEP-FOLD 3), molecular docking (PatchDock), and molecular dynamics simulation.
extractions.finding: Two epitopes predicted — VLMVSAFPL (from RNA polymerase β subunit, 4 HLA DRB1 alleles) and IRYLAALHL (from membrane protein insertase YidC, 6 HLA DRB1 alleles) — with satisfactory docking interactions and population coverage.
extractions.dataset: T. whipplei proteome (5 proteins from NCBI/UniProtKB)
extractions.metric: atomic contact energy (ACE), binding score, RMSD, VaxiJen antigenicity score, 1-log50k affinity, population coverage
extractions.baseline: reference HLA-peptide crystal structures from RCSB-PDB (4AH2, 4IS6)
extractions.result: RMSD 0–1.5 Å, VaxiJen ≥0.94 for both epitopes, ACE -443.3 kcal/mol, satisfactory world population coverage

extractions.problem: Need to characterize B and T cell epitopes of Der f 25, a major allergen from house dust mite D. farinae, to support allergen immunotherapy development.
extractions.method: Bioinformatics-based epitope prediction using DNAStar, BPAP, BepiPred 1.0 (B cell), NetMHCIIpan-3.0 and NetMHCII-2.2 (T cell), with homology modeling (SWISS-MODEL) and structural validation (Ramachandran, ERRAT, VERIFY 3D, ProSA).
extractions.finding: Eight B cell epitopes and five T cell epitopes were predicted for Der f 25, which belongs to the triosephosphate isomerase family with a TPI pattern (PS001371). The homology model was validated as high quality.
extractions.dataset: Der f 25 protein from D. farinae (NCBI KC305500.1), template T. molitor TPI (PDB: 2I9E, 74% identity)
extractions.metric: Ramachandran plot (% favored), ERRAT quality factor, VERIFY 3D, ProSA Z-score, RMSD
extractions.result: 93% Ramachandran favored, ERRAT 97.034, 8 B cell + 5 T cell epitopes predicted

extractions.problem: mRNA levels predict no more than 40% of protein abundance in human cells, and the contribution of translation efficiency to this discrepancy is poorly characterized across cell lines.
extractions.method: Bioinformatic integration of genome-wide mRNA (NGS/FPKM via tophat/cufflinks) and protein (mass spectrometry/IBAQ) levels with protein stability data (pulsed SILAC from HeLa) across five human cell lines to estimate translation efficiency.
extractions.finding: Estimated translation efficiency shows considerable consistency between cell lines for many genes; deviations are attributable to regulated protein degradation or known translational control mechanisms, suggesting improved models for interpreting mRNA expression data are feasible.
extractions.dataset: 5 human cell lines (MCF-7, HeLa, A-431, U-2 OS, U-251 MG), 8,170 genes, 24,920 mRNA-protein pairs
extractions.result: mRNA predicts 24–34% (R²) of protein levels, TE estimated for 3,990 genes, median ~43.6 proteins/mRNA/h
